comment.body:
Futs are strong during asia...
Many likes to still think in bull/bear, up/down duality. Forget it.
As said, it's all about the vol cycles and distribution, especially in times like this.
What did I say? - I said, I'm leaning towards a bounce into OpEx. Bias remains, will explain.
My last post doesn't calcel it out. it describes the key dynamics and drivers of that. 
And as a result one can build his risk management up bcs there is a huge risk as I keep saying. You cannot be blindly bullish or bearish. Why not disperse positions into both ways?

comment.body:
Thats what "twitter traders" can't understand. They would say if they would see that I have march expo puts while playing for calls and calling for short-term bottoming, that "ok, slut, this way you are right in both direction". 
And I would say: "no, you pudding of a being, it's not a who's right game, it's freaking trading. If one can monitor the distribution well and take/size positions in a way that results green at the EOD, he won. It's trading, not 'guess the direction to win a candy' game..." ahh.. :D
I have a bias and I have a certain estimated probability and I size accordingly.

comment.body:
I saw yesterday you like volsigs insights. You can learn from him, maybe the only one out-there who actually knows the market makers hedging dynamics.
but be careful bcs not everything can be derived from these flows as he tries to do. 
sometimes market is driven solely on these dynamics yes, but in some regimes, other flows are there mixed with the dealer flow that is still 60%+ of the daily flows. but they are jsut reflecting and reacting to the customer flows, and customers can play with that... like rolling large itm call positions up in a gamma squeeze etc.
so don't try to think about the market like i f it would be a simulated thing solely dependent on dealer flows
comment.raw_body:

comment.body: you mentioned spot up vol up is bad for liquidity, does it remain the case if we manage to stay above the 5565 critical strike?
comment.raw_body: you mentioned spot up vol up is bad for liquidity, does it remain the case if we manage to stay above the 5565 critical strike?

comment.body:
high vol = low liquidity, in my educational post, i explained why.
so my answer is, only if vol goes up.
look, what i wrote: oscillation, mean reversion trading to compress vol. 
so if vol is compressed, below the JPM strike the vanna flow is suppressive, but into the next weak this will change a bit. 
vol doesn't necessary need to go up. 
from mechanical flow perspective it should, but there are effects that doesn't follow from dealer flows, thats my point one message above

comment.body: Bit of a telltale last night was the size of the after hours trade... i generally look at the 30min after close volume....if you look at last two weeks for SPY you can see Fri 28 9mill, mon 3.5mill,Tue 5.2mill,Wed 3.8mill,Thurs 4.3mill, Fri 3.5mill, Mon 5.0 mill, Tue 4.3mill, wed 4.06 mill, Thurs 9 mill. If you the analyse what happened next two days and measure SPY close versus lowest point you will see that on Friday 28th we had 20 point drop from 592 to 572. The rest of the days when we had much lower after hr volume you had much smaller moves...last night we had another 9 mill so lets see over next two days. It is not the whole story because on days with big move you often see large number of trades reported late the next day. These trades are conducted on the London exchange i believe which means they can be hidden for extra time from markets. Also there could be other reasons for large volume like opex etc...but away from special dates its not a bad rule of thumb...I think these after hrs trades are Market Makers getting their ducks in A row...but i could be wrong on that
